grader_components.part: decay fungi need moisture, oxygen, warmth, and a food source together
exposed end grain atop a post is where rainwater collects longest

grader_missing_subclaim: the exposed end grain atop a post is where rainwater collects longest
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Wood science supplies the mundane reading: decay fungi need moisture, oxygen, mild warmth, and an organic food source together [source].
grader_proofs_verified: Molds, most sapwood stains, and decay are caused by fungi, which are microscopic, thread-like microorganisms that must have organic material to live.
For some of them, wood offers the required food supply.
The growth of fungi depends on suitably mild temperatures, moisture, and air (oxygen).
grader_why: The first component (fungi need moisture, oxygen, warmth, and food together) is fully provable from the source—"The growth of fungi depends on suitably mild temperatures, moisture, and air (oxygen)" plus the food-source sentences—but the two shown sentences only cover temperature and moisture, missing oxygen and food. The second component (end grain atop a post collecting rainwater longest) appears nowhere in the source, making it an unsourced author aside. Because one component is unprovable from the cited text, the author's problem dominates: rewrite or add a separate citation for the end-grain claim.
fable_reasoning: Wood-science core (decay fungi need moisture/oxygen/warmth/food) is provable from the handbook (arbiter confirms oxygen+food present, only moisture/warmth shown). The second component — 'exposed end grain atop a post is where rainwater collects longest' — is nowhere in the source (arbiter). Core proven + one component unsourced -> partial.

grader_components.part: filled with pins, nails, and bodily fluids (urine)
attested from roughly the 1650s to at least 1705

grader_rewrite_suggestion: Early modern English witch bottles were often stoneware (or glass) vessels filled with urine, pins, nails, and hair, then heated and/or buried into the ground, walls, floors, or under the hearth, attested from roughly the mid-seventeenth century to at least 1705.
grader_proofs_verified: This cure followed a variable but mostly standard format, in which urine, from either the afflicted person or an animal, was put in an (often stoneware) vessel, usually with other ingredients that included pins, nails and human hair.
This bottled mixture was then boiled and/or buried into the ground, walls or floors.
This study however considers vessels that were filled with ingredients, heated and sometimes also buried or built into walls and floors, to cure bewitchment in early modern England and New England.
These texts demonstrate that a variety of people understood this practice as a treatment for bewitchment, and used it from the around the mid-seventeenth-century to at least 1705.

Detailing the trial of Elinor Shaw and Mary Phillips, Ralph Davis noted that ‘Mrs Ireland the [bewitched] Boy’s Mother, was advised to Cork up some of his water in a stone Bottle, fill'd up of Pins and Needles, and to Bury it under the Fire Hearth’.
grader_why: The one shown sentence (the OED definition) proves only the stone/glass vessel, the urine/nails contents, and the heating; it says nothing about concealment locations or the date range, and the article actually disputes its 'repelling' framing. Those unshown components are recoverable from the source (mid-17th c.-to-1705 dating, and burial in ground/walls/floors/under the hearth), but 'concealed near thresholds' is not: in this source only horseshoes are nailed to thresholds, while the bottles are buried in floors, walls, or under the hearth. Because a subclaim (thresholds) is not provable from the source at all, the author must fix it, so add_citation_or_rewrite governs despite the other components being fetchable.
fable_reasoning: OED definition proves material (stone/glass), contents (urine/nails/hair), and heating — but the load-bearing specifics fail: 'pins', the '1650s to 1705' dating, and witch-bottle concealment 'near thresholds' are unproven. thwaite2020 does contain 'threshold' but only re nailing horseshoes/heated objects to door-thresholds, not witch-bottle concealment; the source places bottles in ground/walls/floors and under a hearth (hearths yes, thresholds no). Distinctive placement+dating unproven -> unsupported.

grader_components.part: placing charged objects at a dwelling's vulnerable edges (walls, foundations, boundaries)

grader_proofs_verified: We suggest archaeologists consider these purification and apotropaic magic techniques of closure in the formation processes they encounter. For example, scholars working in similar contexts containing burned houses beyond the Southwest such as the Pre-Pottery Neolithic Middle East (e.g., Banning, ; Finlayson et al., ); the Burned House Horizon (Chapman, ; Chapman & Bisserka, ); Neolithic Ireland (Smyth, ); Formative Mesoamerica (Flannery, :16–24); and Agro-pastoral Periods of northwest Argentina (Gordillo & Leiton, ; Nielsen & Walker, ) may encounter analogous manipulations of animate materials.
Similarly, Acoma priests push arrow points into walls, strengthening them by transferring the lightning anima of the projectiles into the building (Parsons,

For example, van Gennep ( :15–25) remarks that laying of foundation deposits are liminal rites that remove existing and future dangers or taboos of a new home, allowing for a family to change residence.
Historical archaeologists (e.g., Hoggard, ) have documented the prophylactic concealment of witch bottles, cats, pipes, shoes, and other objects within European and North American buildings.

Before the reenactment of this mythical battle, the war society chiefs walk the town pushing their arrow points into the walls of all the houses to give them strength; their allied Katsina spirits also similarly touch the walls with their wooden staffs.
grader_why: The single shown sentence only states that apotropaic magic contributes to closure strata and does not prove the claim's substance. However, the source itself supplies every component: it names apotropaic magic, asserts its cross-cultural recurrence across Neolithic/Formative contexts worldwide, and repeatedly documents charged objects (arrow points, foundation deposits, concealed witch bottles) placed at a dwelling's walls, foundations, and boundaries. Because the proof exists in the source but was not the sentence displayed, this is a tool problem.
fable_reasoning: Walker2022 does support apotropaic magic + protective object placement (ritual closure in pueblos) — grep confirms apotropaic/ritual-closure/pueblo/magic in the source, and arbiter agrees object-placement and cross-cultural recurrence are supported. The distinctive framing 'placing charged objects at a dwelling's vulnerable edges' is not in this single source. Substantive core supported, framing over-read -> partial, not a full unsupported. Pipeline's unsupported under-credits the proven core.

grader_components.part: Finnish tradition of hiding animal bones, coins, and tools in thresholds
witchcraft fears more pronounced in densely built towns than in looser settlements

grader_proofs_verified: Social stress and concerns about envious witchcraft must have been more pronounced in densely built towns and group villages than in the looser settlements.
Threshold locations and sharp metal tools are most often connected to apotropaic concealments intended to strengthen borders.
When this older threshold stone was removed, the tusk of a pig was found under it (Appx. 3: 44).
grader_why: The one shown sentence is tangential (east/west persistence and witchcraft-protection as a later development) and proves none of the three components. Yet the source states almost verbatim that witchcraft concerns were 'more pronounced in densely built towns and group villages than in the looser settlements' and connects thresholds with protective concealments of sharp tools and animal remains. Because the proof exists in the source but was not displayed, the tool must fetch the correct sentences rather than the author fixing the claim.
fable_reasoning: The dense-vs-loose-settlement component is proven verbatim in the shown evidence. Arbiter (wrong_or_insufficient_evidence) states the threshold-hiding component (pig tusk under a threshold, coin in a doorjamb, metal tools) IS in the source but was not shown, so the full claim is provable. Source could not be grepped (PDF), so I trust the arbiter's specific artifact citations -> supported, low confidence given the owner dispute.

grader_components.part: English museum surveys (exist, survey English examples)
survey reports concealment pattern at hearths
survey reports concealment pattern at doorways

grader_missing_subclaim: that the English museum survey reports concealment at doorways — MOLA (the survey) reports 'hearths or beneath the floors,' not doorways; doorways concealment appears only in the Historic England blog, which is not a museum survey
grader_rewrite_suggestion: English museum surveys report a comparable concealment pattern at hearths and beneath the floors of historic buildings.
grader_proofs_verified: They have been found concealed in a range of contexts: placed in hearths or beneath the floors of historic buildings, or on archaeological sites, in churchyards, ditches and riverbanks.

For the first time, all known examples that survive in museums and other collections around Southern and Eastern England (the apparent geographical extent of the phenomenon) are to be surveyed first hand or through literature review and critiqued along with their contents.
grader_why: The museum survey (MOLA) and the hearths concealment pattern are both provable from the source and merely shown with the wrong sentences, but the 'doorways' element is not reported by any museum survey: MOLA lists hearths and beneath-floors only, and doorways appears solely in the Historic England general-audience article, which is not a survey. Because the claim attributes the 'hearths and doorways' pattern specifically to museum surveys, that scope is an author overreach that must be rewritten or separately cited. Rewriting to 'hearths and beneath the floors' matches what the surveyed examples actually document.
fable_reasoning: False-unsupported: grep-confirmed the proof in both cited sources. historicengland2016: witch bottles 'sometimes found buried beneath hearths or doorways in old houses'; mola_witchbottles: 'They have been found concealed in a range of contexts: placed in hearths or beneath the floors' and it surveys 'all known examples that survive in museums'. Museum surveys + concealment at hearths/doorways both proven -> supported. Pipeline showed the wrong sentences (witches' marks + survey scope) and missed the retrievable proof.
